content:
# Revelation Chapter 18 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 290-291 | **Source Image:** REV_018_page_001.jpg, REV_018_page_002.jpg

1. After these things I saw another angel coming down out of heaven, having great authority, and the earth was illuminated by his glory.

2. And he cried out with a mighty voice, saying, "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great, and she has become a dwelling place of demons and a haunt of every unclean spirit and a haunt of every unclean and hateful bird.

3. For from the wine of the wrath of her fornication all the nations have fallen, and the kings of the earth committed fornication with her, and the merchants of the earth became rich from the power of her luxury."

4. And I heard another voice from heaven, saying, "Come out of her, my people, so that you do not share in her sins, and so that you do not receive of her plagues;

5. for her sins have been heaped up as far as heaven, and God has remembered her iniquities.

6. Render to her as she herself also rendered, and repay double according to her works; in the cup which she mixed, mix double for her.

7. To the degree that she glorified herself and lived in luxury, to that degree give her torment and mourning. For in her heart she says, 'I sit as a queen and am not a widow, and I will never see mourning.'

8. For this reason in one day her plagues will come -- death and mourning and famine -- and she will be burned with fire; for strong is the Lord God who has judged her."

9. And the kings of the earth, who committed fornication and lived in luxury with her, will weep and mourn over her when they see the smoke of her burning,

10. standing at a distance because of the fear of her torment, saying, "Woe, woe, the great city, Babylon, the strong city, for in one hour your judgment has come."

11. And the merchants of the earth weep and mourn over her, because no one buys their cargo anymore --

12. cargo of gold and silver and precious stone and pearls, and fine linen and purple and silk and scarlet, and every kind of citron wood, and every article of ivory, and every article of most precious wood and of bronze and of iron and of marble,

13. and cinnamon and spice and incense and myrrh and frankincense, and wine and oil and fine flour and wheat, and cattle and sheep, and cargo of horses and chariots and bodies and souls of men.

14. And the fruit of the desire of your soul has departed from you, and all the luxurious and splendid things have perished from you, and they will never find them again.

15. The merchants of these things, who became rich from her, will stand at a distance because of the fear of her torment, weeping and mourning,

16. saying, "Woe, woe, the great city, she who was clothed in fine linen and purple and scarlet, and adorned with gold and precious stone and pearls;

17. for in one hour such great wealth has been laid waste." And every shipmaster and everyone who sails to a place, and sailors and as many as work the sea, stood at a distance,

18. and were crying out as they saw the smoke of her burning, saying, "What city is like the great city?"

19. And they threw dust on their heads and were crying out, weeping and mourning, saying, "Woe, woe, the great city, in which all who had ships in the sea became rich from her wealth, for in one hour she has been laid waste."

20. "Rejoice over her, O heaven, and you saints and apostles and prophets, for God has pronounced judgment for you against her."

21. And one strong angel took up a stone like a great millstone and threw it into the sea, saying, "Thus with violence will Babylon the great city be thrown down, and will be found no more.

22. And the sound of harpists and musicians and flute players and trumpeters will not be heard in you any longer; and no craftsman of any craft will be found in you any longer; and the sound of a mill will not be heard in you any longer;

23. and the light of a lamp will not shine in you any longer; and the voice of a bridegroom and of a bride will not be heard in you any longer; for your merchants were the great ones of the earth, for by your sorcery all the nations were deceived.

24. And in her was found the blood of prophets and of saints and of all who have been slain upon the earth."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

The following verses achieved substantial agreement across all three translators:

- **18:9** -- All three agreed on the basic structure of the kings weeping and mourning upon seeing the smoke of Babylon's burning.
- **18:11** -- Universal agreement that the merchants weep because no one buys their cargo anymore.
- **18:12** -- The cargo list is rendered with near-identical content across all three, differing only in minor phrasing (commas vs. "and").
- **18:18** -- All three agreed on the rhetorical question: "What city is like the great city?"
- **18:19** -- All three agreed on the dust-casting lament with the "woe, woe" / "how terrible" refrain and the one-hour desolation.
- **18:22-23** -- The "never again" litany of harpists, craftsmen, millstone, lamp, and bride/bridegroom was rendered with strong agreement across all three translators.

---
## Disagreement Log

**Verse 1:**
- EMET: "coming down from the heaven, having great authority, and the earth was illuminated from the glory of him"
- LOGOS: "coming down out of heaven, having great authority, and the earth was illuminated by his glory"
- PNEUMA: "coming down from heaven, invested with great authority, and the earth blazed with his glory"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Out of heaven" for *ek tou ouranou*. "Illuminated by his glory" is faithful to *ephotisthe... ek tes doxes autou*. PNEUMA's "blazed" is interpretive; "invested with" adds a nuance not in the Greek.

**Verse 2:**
- EMET: "a prison of every unclean spirit and a prison of every unclean and hated bird"
- LOGOS: "a haunt of every unclean spirit and a haunt of every unclean and hateful bird"
- PNEUMA: "a refuge for every foul spirit, a cage for every unclean and detestable bird"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "haunt" (LOGOS) for both instances of *phylake*. While EMET's "prison" is the literal meaning, "haunt" better conveys the desolation context. PNEUMA uses three different terms ("haunt," "refuge," "cage") where the Greek uses one word (*phylake*) consistently. "Hateful" (LOGOS) for *memisemenou* over "hated" (EMET) or "detestable" (PNEUMA).

**Verse 3:**
- EMET: "all the nations have drunk" (*peptokan* read as "drunk")
- LOGOS: "all the nations have fallen"
- PNEUMA: "all the nations have drunk the maddening wine"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "have fallen." The Sinaiticus reads *peptokan* ("have fallen"), not *pepokan* ("have drunk"), as LOGOS correctly notes. This echoes the "fallen, fallen" of verse 2. PNEUMA's "maddening" is an interpretive addition.

**Verse 4:**
- EMET: "Come out from her, the people of me, that you may not share in the sins of her"
- LOGOS: "Come out of her, my people, so that you do not participate in her sins"
- PNEUMA: "Come out of her, my people, so that you do not share in her sins"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA blends LOGOS/PNEUMA: "Come out of her, my people, so that you do not share in her sins." "Share" (PNEUMA) is slightly more natural than "participate" (LOGOS), and both are faithful to *synkoinonesete*.

**Verse 5:**
- EMET: "the sins of her were joined together unto the heaven, and the God remembered the unrighteous [deeds] of her"
- LOGOS: "her sins have been heaped up as far as heaven, and God has remembered her iniquities"
- PNEUMA: "her sins have piled up as high as heaven, and God has remembered her crimes"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Heaped up" captures *ekollethesan* (literally "joined/glued together") in context. "Iniquities" (LOGOS) for *adikemata* over "unrighteous deeds" (EMET) or "crimes" (PNEUMA).

**Verse 6:**
- EMET: "Give back to her as also she gave back, and double the double [things] according to the works of her"
- LOGOS: "Render to her as she herself also rendered, and repay double according to her works"
- PNEUMA: "Pay her back exactly as she has paid others. Give her double for everything she has done"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Render" is appropriately formal for *apodote*. "Repay double" smoothly captures *diplosate ta dipla*. PNEUMA's paraphrase departs too far; EMET's "double the double things" is awkwardly literal.

**Verse 7:**
- EMET: "As much as she glorified herself and lived in luxury, so much torment and mourning give to her"
- LOGOS: "To the degree that she glorified herself and lived in luxury, to that degree give her torment and mourning"
- PNEUMA: "To the same degree she glorified herself and wallowed in luxury, give her that same measure of torment and grief"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "To the degree... to that degree" is elegant and accurate. PNEUMA's "wallowed" is pejorative beyond the Greek *estreniasein*. "Mourning" (EMET/LOGOS) over "grief" (PNEUMA) for *penthos*.

**Verse 8:**
- EMET: "strong [is the] Lord the God, the [one] having judged her"
- LOGOS: "strong is the Lord God who judges her"
- PNEUMA: "mighty is the Lord God who has pronounced judgment on her"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "strong is the Lord God who has judged her." "Strong" (EMET/LOGOS) for *ischyros* over "mighty" (PNEUMA). The Sinaiticus aorist participle *krinas* ("having judged") indicates completed action, so "has judged" (aligning with EMET's note) is preferred over LOGOS's present tense "judges."

**Verse 10:**
- EMET: "Woe, woe, the great city, Babylon the strong city, because in one hour the judgment of you came"
- LOGOS: "Woe, woe, the great city, Babylon, the strong city, for in one hour your judgment has come"
- PNEUMA: "How terrible! How terrible, O great city! O Babylon, city of power! In just one hour your doom has come!"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Woe, woe" for *ouai ouai* is the established translation; PNEUMA's "How terrible!" departs from the traditional rendering. "Judgment" (EMET/LOGOS) over "doom" (PNEUMA) for *krisis*.

**Verse 13:**
- EMET: "and of bodies and souls of men"
- LOGOS: "and bodies and souls of men"
- PNEUMA: "and human bodies, that is, human souls"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "and cargo of horses and chariots and bodies and souls of men." PNEUMA's "that is" is an interpretive gloss not in the Greek. The text lists *somaton kai psychas anthropon* as separate items in the cargo list.

**Verse 14:**
- EMET: "the fruit of the desire of the soul of you departed from you, and all the fat [things] and the splendid [things] perished"
- LOGOS: "the fruit of the desire of your soul has departed from you, and all the luxurious and splendid things have perished"
- PNEUMA: "The ripe fruit your soul craved has vanished from you. All your glittering luxury and splendor have perished"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Luxurious" (LOGOS) for *lipara* over "fat" (EMET) or "glittering luxury" (PNEUMA). "Departed" (EMET/LOGOS) over "vanished" (PNEUMA) for *apelthen*.

**Verse 16:**
- EMET: "having been clothed in fine linen... having been gilded in gold"
- LOGOS: "she who was clothed in fine linen... and adorned with gold"
- PNEUMA: "dressed in fine linen... adorned with gold"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Clothed" and "adorned" are accurate and natural.

**Verse 17:**
- EMET: "everyone sailing upon a place"
- LOGOS: "every one who sails to a place"
- PNEUMA: "every coastal traveler"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. The Sinaiticus reading *epi topon pleon* is unusual; LOGOS preserves it faithfully as "who sails to a place." PNEUMA's "coastal traveler" is too interpretive.

**Verse 20:**
- EMET: "the God judged the judgment of you from her"
- LOGOS: "God has pronounced judgment for you against her"
- PNEUMA: "God has handed down the verdict against her on your behalf"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Pronounced judgment for you against her" captures the cognate *ekrinen to krima* construction clearly. PNEUMA's "handed down the verdict" is interpretive; EMET's literal rendering is unclear.

**Verse 21:**
- EMET: "one strong angel took up a stone like a great millstone, and cast [it] into the sea"
- LOGOS: "one strong angel took up a stone like a great millstone and threw it into the sea"
- PNEUMA: "a mighty angel picked up a boulder the size of a great millstone and hurled it into the sea"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Threw" for *ebalen* over "cast" (EMET, archaic) or "hurled" (PNEUMA, intensified). "Stone" (EMET/LOGOS) for *lithon* over "boulder" (PNEUMA).

**Verse 24:**
- EMET: "of holy [ones]"
- LOGOS: "of saints"
- PNEUMA: "of God's holy people"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "saints" (LOGOS) for *hagion*. PNEUMA's "God's holy people" adds "God's" not present in the Greek.

-- SOPHIA
